content:
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="judices_p154_l1">,
								i.e. the members of a special court (<foreign xml:lang="lat">quaestio</foreign>)
								established by the <foreign xml:lang="lat">Lex Papia</foreign> (see
								Introd. to the oration, p.154) to inquire into cases arising under that
								law. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="versatum_p154_l3">:
								cf. the date of the defence of Roscius and the opening passage of that
								oration. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hujusce_rei_p154_l3">,
								i.e. <foreign xml:lang="lat">dicendi</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ratio_p155_l1">, <gloss>
								knowledge</gloss>, i.e. <gloss>theoretic acquaintance</gloss>,
								contrasted with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="exercitatio_p155_l1">
								, <gloss>practice</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="A_Licinius_p155_l3">:
								following the custom of naturalized foreigners, as well as freedmen,
								Archias had taken the gentile name of his noble friends and patrons, the
								Luculli. Cicero's motive in always speaking of him by his Roman name is
								obvious. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="inde_usque_p155_l6">
								, <gloss>from as far back as that I say</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="principem_p155_l7">
								, <gloss>master</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="rationem_p155_l8">
								, <gloss>course</gloss>. For the connection of the meanings of this
								word, see Vocab. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="a_quo_p155_l10">
								relates to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="huic_p155_l10">,
								which is dat. after </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ferre_p155_l10">; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quo_p155_l10">
								relates to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="id_p155_l10">: <gloss>surely,
								to the man himself from whom I have received that whereby</gloss>, etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ceteris_p155_l11">, <gloss>all
								the rest</gloss> [of my fellow-citizens], i.e. other than Archias. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="alios_p155_l11">, <gloss>some
								of them</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="opem_p155_l12">
								corresponds to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="opitulari_p155_l12">
								; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="salutem_p155_l12">,
								to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="servare_p155_l12">. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="neque_p155_l14">, <gloss>and
								not</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="dicendi_disciplina_p155_l15">
								, <gloss>art or science of oratory</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ne_quidem_p155_l15">,
								etc., i.e. not even I, though by profession an orator, have devoted
								myself to oratory alone. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quaestione_legitima_p155_l20">:
								see note on <foreign xml:lang="lat">judices</foreign>, p.154, 1. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="severissimos_p155_l22">,
								i.e. before men of the old Roman stamp, who might not be favorably
								impressed by such praise of literature. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="forensi_sermone_p155_l24">
								is not used here in its special meaning, "the language of the courts,"
								but in its wider sense, <gloss>the language of the Forum</gloss>, i.e.
								the ordinary tone of practical affairs (the Forum being the <foreign xml:lang="lat">centre</foreign> of Roman business and politics). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_patiamini_p155_l27">,
								a purp. clause in appos. with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="veniam_p155_l27">
								(1.25). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hoc_concursu_p155_l28">,
								locat. abl. expressing the circumstances; so also </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hac_humanitate_p155_l28">
								, <gloss>with men of your cultivation</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hoc_praetore_p155_l29">:
								Q. Cicero was himself a poet and man of critical taste. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="paulo_liberius_p155_l30">
								, <gloss>with somewhat unusual freedom: § </gloss>29 1, a (93, a); B.
								240,1; G. 297; H. 498 (444,1); H-B. 241,2. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="otium_studium_p155_l31">
								, <gloss>a quiet life of study</gloss> (almost hendiadys); so </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="judiciis_periculisque_p155_l31">
								below. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cum_p156_l3">:
								causal, but best translated <gloss>when</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="esset_p156_l4">:
								for tense, see § <ref n="AG 589">589</ref>, b, I (337, b, I); B. 268,
								4; G. 597, R.4; H. 647(527, iii); H.-B. 581, b, N. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="asciscendum_fuisse_p156_l5">:
								in the direct, <foreign xml:lang="lat">asciscendus erat</foreign>; § <ref n="AG 517">517</ref>, d (308,d); B. 304,3, b; G. 597,R.3,a; H.
								582(511,2); H-B. 581,a; cf. note on Pompey's Military Command, sect. <ref n="Cic. Man. 50">50</ref> (<ref n="pl88.32">p. 88, l. 32</ref>), <foreign xml:lang="lat">erat deligendus</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="urbe_p156_l9">: § <ref n="AG 282">282</ref>,d(184,c); G. 411, R.3; H. 393,7(363,4 2); H-B.
								452, a. . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="contigit_p156_l12">,
								sc. <foreign xml:lang="lat">ei</foreign>, i.e. Archias. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="post_p156_l12">, <gloss>
								afterwards</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tunc_p156_l15">, <gloss>at
								that time</gloss>. This was the long period of comparative quiet between
								the Gracchan disturbances (<date from="-0133" to="-0121">
									<date when="-0133">B.C. 133</date>-121</date>) and the tribunate of
								Drusus (<date when="-0091">B.C. 91</date>), which was followed by the
								Social War and the civil wars of Marius and Sulla. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Latio_p156_l16">:
								not the geographical Latium merely, but all towns which at that time
								possessed Latin citizenship; that is, the Latin colonies, such as
								Venusia, the birthplace of the poet Horace. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="de_ingeniis_p156_l21">,
								i.e. could form some opinion about the talents of literary men. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="absentibus_p156_l23">
								, <gloss>people at a distance</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Mario_Catulo_p156_l23">
								(coss. <date when="-0102">B.C. 102</date>); of these, Marius was
								renowned for his exploits, while Catulus was a good officer, and also a
								man of culture. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nactus_est_p156_l25">,
								etc., <gloss>he happened to find holding the consulship.</gloss>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="eos_alter_p156_l25">
								, <gloss>men of such a kind that one of them</gloss>, etc. This would
								not only furnish him with themes for his poetry but insure appreciation
								of his genius. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Luculli_p156_l27">:
								Lucius, the one who fought against Mithridates, and his brother Marcus;
								both of them belonged to the highest ranks of the aristocracy, and were
								men of distinguished taste and culture. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ingeni_p156_l29">,
								pred. gen. after an understood <foreign xml:lang="lat">erat</foreign>: <gloss>this
								was</gloss> [a proof] <gloss>not only of his genius</gloss>, etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_esset_p156_l30">,
								result clause in app. with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hoc_p156_l30">
								(l.29). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="jucundus_p157_l1">,
								etc.: ever since the introduction of Greek culture at Rome, it had been
								customary for cultivated Romans of high rank to entertain Greek men of
								letters in their houses, partly as tutors and partly as companions. Such
								associates frequently accompanied their patrons on their journeys and
								even on their campaigns. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Metello_Numidico_p157_l1">:
								the most distinguished member of this family. He was predecessor of
								Marius in the war against Jugurtha, and from this service in Numidia
								received his <foreign xml:lang="lat">agnomen</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Aemilio_p157_l2">,
								i.e. M. Aemilius Scaurus (cos. <date when="-0115">B.C. 115</date>), for
								many years <foreign xml:lang="lat">princeps senatus</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Catulo_p157_l3">:
								see note on <ref n="pl156.23">p. 156, l. 23</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="L_Crasso_p157_l3">:
								the most distinguished orator of his time, a man of genius and culture
								(see Introd., <ref n="intro. 2">ch. ii, p. xxxiv</ref>); he died <date when="-0091">B.C. 91</date>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Drusum_p157_l4">
								(M. Livius), tribune <date when="-0091">B.C. 91</date>, a distinguished
								orator and statesman, who lost his life in a vain attempt to reconcile
								the aristocratic and democratic factions in the republic. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Octavios_p157_l4">:
								see Cat. 3, sect. <ref n="Cic. Catil. 3.24">24</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Catonem_p157_l4">:
								probably the father of the famous Cato of Utica is meant. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Hortensiorum_p157_l5">:
								the most eminent of these was Q. Hortensius, the rival of Cicero and his
								opponent in the case of Verres. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_forte_p157_l8">
								, <gloss>those (if there were any) who</gloss>, etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Heracliam_p157_l11">:
								an important Greek city on the southern coast of Lucania. In the war
								with Pyrrhus it had fought on the side of the Romans, and <date when="-0278">B.C. 278</date> it entered into an alliance of the
								closest and most favorable character (</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="aequissimo_foedere_p157_l11">
								). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Silvani_lege_p157_l15">,
								etc., i.e. the <foreign xml:lang="lat">Lex Plautia-Papiria</foreign>, of
								the tribunes M. Plautius Silvanus and C. Papirius Carbo (not to be
								confounded with his infamous cousin Cneius, the Marian leader after the
								death of Cinna), extended the Roman citizenship to all Italian
								communities which had not yet received it. These towns now exchanged
								their independence for Roman citizenship, and became incorporated with
								the republic; though many of them, as Heraclia, hesitated about making
								the change, and did it with great reluctance. They lost all rights of
								independent government (such as that of coining money, the <foreign xml:lang="lat">jus exsili</foreign>, etc.). Latin became the
								official language; justice was administered by Roman law; and in most
								cases their government was organized on the model of Rome, having <foreign xml:lang="lat">duumviri</foreign>,for consuls, and a <foreign xml:lang="lat">curia</foreign> for the Senate. The passage here
								given from the Plautian-Papirian Law contains its application to
								citizens of foreign birth, like Archias. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_qui_p157_l15">,
								etc.: the law is quoted in indir. disc., but the main clause is omitted,
								being implied in </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="data_est_p157_l15">;
								see § <ref n="AG 592">592</ref>, 2 (341, c) ; G. 663, 2, b; H.-B. 535,
								I, a. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="essent_professi_p157_l18">
								, <gloss>should have declared their intention</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Q_Metellum_p157_l19">
								[<foreign xml:lang="lat">Pium</foreign>], praetor, <date when="-0089">B.C.
								89</date>: the most eminent living member of this family, and one of the
								leaders of the aristocracy. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Grati_p157_l22">,
								the complainant (see Introd., p.154 of text). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="religione_p158_l1">
								, <gloss>conscientiousness</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="desideras_p158_l6">
								, <gloss>call for</gloss> (lit. <gloss>miss, feel the want of</gloss>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Italico_bello_p158_l6">,
								i.e. the Social War: § <ref n="AG 424">424</ref>, d (259, a); G. 394,
								R.; H. 486 (429); H-B. 439, a, 3. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="municipi_p158_l11">:
								since the bestowal of the Roman citizenship, the Italian <foreign xml:lang="lat">civitates</foreign> had become Roman <foreign xml:lang="lat">municipia</foreign> (see Introd., p. liii). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="idem_p158_l12">, <gloss>you
								yourself</gloss> (lit. <gloss>the same man</gloss>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="domicilium_p158_15">
								and </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="professio_p158_15">
								). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="civitatem_datam_p158_15">,
								i.e. by the law before cited. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="professione_p158_l17">
								, <gloss>list of declarations</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="conlegio_p158_l18">:
								the praetors, when regarded as a whole, could be spoken of as a "board." </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cum_p158_l19">, <gloss>
								while</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Appi_p158_l19">,
								i.e. Appius Claudius, husband of Caecilia (the friend of Roscius: see
								Rosc. Am., sect. <ref n="Cic. S. Rosc. 50">50</ref>) and father of the
								infamous Clodius. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Gabini_p158_l20">:
								see Introd. to Pompey's Military Command. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="damnationem_p158_l21">:
								he was condemned, <date when="-0054">B.C. 54</date>, for extortion on
								complaint of the Achaeans. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="L_Lentulum_p158_l23">:
								nothing further is known of him; he probably presided over a court (<foreign xml:lang="lat">judices</foreign>) to determine cases involving
								citizenship under the new law. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="multis_p158_l29">
								and </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="praeditis_p158_l29">
								are dat. after </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="impertiebant_p158_l29">; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="arte_p158_l29">,
								abl. after </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="praeditis_p158_l29_2">
								. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Graecia_p158_l30">,
								i.e. Magna Graecia, the Greek cities of Italy. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="credo_p158_l31">
								(ironical), <gloss>I suppose</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Locrensis_p158_l31">:
								Locri Epizephyrii, a Greek city near Rhegium. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_p158_l32">
								relates to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="id_p158_l32">,
								which is governed by </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="largiri_p158_l32">
								understood. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ingeni_p159_l1">
								limits </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="gloria_p159_l1">,
								which depends on </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="praedito_p159_l1">
								. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="civitatem_datam_p159_l2">,
								i.e. by the <foreign xml:lang="lat">Lex Plautia-Papiria</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="legem_Papiam_p159_l3">:
								see Introd. to the oration. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="illis_p159_l4">,
								sc. <foreign xml:lang="lat">tabulis</foreign>, i.e. of Tarentum,
								Rhegium, and Naples. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="census_p159_l6">:
								the lists of citizens made out by the censors for purposes of taxation.
								These were, of course, excellent evidence on a question of citizenship;
								but they were not needed in this case. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="requiris_p159_l6">:
								cf. <foreign xml:lang="lat">desideras</foreign> in the same sense in
								sect. <ref n="Cic. Arch. 8">8</ref> (<ref n="pl158.6">p. 158, l. 6</ref>
								). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="est_obscurum_p159_l6">
								(ironical), <gloss>it is not generally known</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="proximis_p159_l7">,
								abl. of time: translate by <gloss>under</gloss>. The censors referred to
								were L. Gellius and Cn. Lentulus (<date when="-0070">B.C. 70</date>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="clarissimo_p159_l7">:
								observe the art with which Cicero here again calls attention to the
								connection of Archias with the distinguished Romans any one of whom
								could at any moment have procured him the citizenship if he had not
								already possessed it. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="apud_exercitum_p159_l8">,
								i.e. in the war against Mithridates (see Pompey's Military Command). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="superioribus_p159_l8">,
								sc. <foreign xml:lang="lat">censoribus</foreign>. New censors were
								regularly appointed every five years; those here referred to were Q.
								Marcius Philippus and M. Perperna (<date when="-0086">B.C. 86</date>).
								In the present instance the succession had been interfered with by
								Sulla, but restored in <date when="-0070">B.C. 70</date>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_Asia_p159_l9">:
								this was in the First Mithridatic War, in which Lucullus served as <foreign xml:lang="lat">quaestor</foreign> to Sulla. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="primis_p159_l9">,
								i.e. the first after the passage of the <foreign xml:lang="lat">lex
								Plautia-Papiria</foreign>: these were L. Julius Caesar and P. Crassus (<date when="-0089">B.C. 89</date>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="esse_versatum_p159_l14">
								(sc. <foreign xml:lang="lat">eum</foreign>), <gloss>had availed himself
								of</gloss>: this clause is the obj. of <foreign xml:lang="lat">
								criminaris</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="testamentum_p159_l14">,
								etc., acts which no foreigner could do. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_beneficiis_p159_l16">,
								etc., <gloss>his name was reported for a reward from the state</gloss>
								(i.e. on the ground of some special merit); this, of course, implied
								citizenship. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="suo_p159_l18">,
								etc., i.e. Archias and his friends knew that he was a citizen and had
								acted as such, whatever might be said on the other side. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ubi_p159_l20"> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">locum ubi</foreign>) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="_reficiatur_p159_l20">,
								rel. clause of purpose. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="suppetere_p159_l22">
								has for subject the suppressed antecedent of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_p159_l22">. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="posse_p159_l22">
								(with a fut. force), <gloss>should be able</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="contentionem_p159_l24">
								, <gloss>strain</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ego_p159_l25">
								(emphat.), etc., <gloss>for my part I admit</gloss> etc. We should
								remember that the more old-fashioned of Cicero's contemporaries were
								still inclined to regard literary and artistic pursuits as frivolous in
								comparison with the more "truly Roman" professions of war and politics
								(cf. <ref n="Verg. A. 6.847">Aeneid, 6. 847</ref> ff.). Hence it was
								important for Cicero to show that literature was of practical value to
								the man of affairs. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="his_studiis_p159_l26">,
								the study of letters in general, including all varieties of literature,
								poetry as well as prose. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nullius_tempore_p159_l30">,
								etc., <gloss>the necessities or interests of no one</gloss> (i.e. as a
								client). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ceteris_p160_l1">
								depends on </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="conceditur_p160_l1">
								(l. 4). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ad_requiem_p160_l3">
								, <gloss>even to repose</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="temporum_p160_l4">
								limits </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quantum_p160_l4">
								(1.3), which has </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tantum_p160_l4">
								(l.6) for antecedent. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="alii_p160_l4">:
								notice how this differs in meaning from </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ceteris_p160_l4">,
								the first-mentioned pursuits (attending to business, celebrating
								festivals, etc.) are common to everybody, the last (being dissipations)
								belong only to "some people." </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tempestivis_conviviis_p160_l4">
								, <gloss>early dinners</gloss>, i.e. beginning by daylight, or in
								business hours, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="a_idleness_p160_l4">:
								we should refer to "late dinners." </gloss>
lemma: a mark of luxury and idleness

content:
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="oratio_facultas_p160_l8">:
								hendiadys. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quantacumque_p160_l8">,
								etc., i.e. <gloss>such as I have</gloss> (a modest disclaimer). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="periculis_p160_l9">:
								Cicero prided himself on defending cases rather than acting for the
								prosecution (cf. <foreign xml:lang="lat">amicorum temporibus</foreign>,
								etc., Pompey's Military Command, sect. <ref n="Cic. Man. 1">1</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quae_p160_l10">,
								i.e. the mere ability to speak. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="illa_p160_l10">
								(obj. of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hauriam_p160_l10">),
								i.e. the moral character resulting from the </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="praecepta_p160_l10">
								mentioned below. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="multorum_p160_l12">,
								i.e. great minds whose thoughts have found expression in literature. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="multis_litteris_p160_l12">
								, <gloss>wide reading</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nihil_esse_p160_l13">,
								etc., these doctrines had been the commonplaces of philosophy and
								letters for hundreds of years before Cicero wrote, and to the cultivated
								Roman they took the place which with us belongs to the ethical teachings
								of sacred literature. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="parvi_p160_l16">, <gloss>of
								slight account</gloss>: § <ref n="AG 417">417</ref> (252, a); B.
								169,4; G. 379, 380, 1; H. 448 (404); H.-B. 356, 1. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="exemplorum_p160_l19">,
								i.e. examples of heroism and virtue recorded in literature. The moral
								education of the ancients consisted largely in the study of the lives of
								eminent men of past ages. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="accederet_p160_l21">
								, <gloss>were thrown upon them</gloss>.. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ex_hoc_p161_l9">,
								etc.: Cicero enumerates the most distinguished patrons of the newly
								introduced Greek culture. Cato is separated from the rest because he was
								in theory opposed to this tendency on account of its imagined ill
								effects; hence the rather apologetic tone in which Cicero speaks of him. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Africanum_p161_l10">:
								Scipio the younger (Aemilianus). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Laelium_p161_l10">:
								the younger Laelius (surnamed <foreign xml:lang="lat">Sapiens</foreign>),
								whose friendship with Scipio Aemilianus forms the groundwork of Cicero's
								famous treatise <title xml:lang="lat">De Amicitia</title>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Furium_p161_l10">:
								L. Furius Philus (cos. <date when="-0136">B.C. 136</date>), a patron of
								literature. </gloss> These three men belonged to the so-called Scipionic Circle, which was
							especially influential in the introduction of Greek culture.

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Catonem_p161_l12">:
								M. Porcius Cato, called the Censor, was one of the leading men of Rome
								in the first half of the second century B.C.: a shrewd, hard-headed
								Roman of the old school, full of prejudices, and priding himself on his
								blunt manners. He was a distinguished antiquarian, and wrote books on
								antiquities and agriculture. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="senem_p161_l13">:
								he gives the name to Cicero's dialogue <title>on Old Age</title> (Cato
								Major). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_si_p161_l15_23">,
								etc.: even if literature, Cicero argues, had no great practical or
								ethical value (as it has), it would still be worthy of respect as a
								means of mental refreshment and diversion. The passage is a very famous
								tribute to liberal studies. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ceterae_p161_l18">,
								sc. <foreign xml:lang="lat">animi adversiones</foreign> (from 1.17). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="adversis_p161_l21">
								[<foreign xml:lang="lat">rebus</foreign>], dat. with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="praebent_p161_l21">
								. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="deberemus_p161_l25">:
								§ <ref n="AG 517">517</ref>, c, N.1 (308, C, N.1); B. 304, 3, a, N. ;
								G. 597, R.3, b; Cf. H. 583 (511, I, N.3) ; H.-B. 582, 3, a. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="videremus_p161_l26">:
								subj. because an integral part of the cont. to fact <foreign xml:lang="lat">apodosis</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Rosci_p161_l27">:
								Q. Roscius, the most eminent actor of his time, defended by Cicero in a
								speech which is still extant. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="corporis_p161_l30">:
								observe the emphatic position as opposed to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="animorum_p161_l30">
								(l. 31). In the ancient drama the action was much more important as
								compared with the delivery and facial expression than is the case on the
								modern stage. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="novo_genere_p162_l2">:
								such praise of letters was, of course, an innovation on the formal
								proceedings of a Roman court. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quotiens_p162_l2">,
								etc.: given as a remarkable instance of the poetical improvisation
								illustrating the </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="celeritas_p162_l2">
								mentioned in § <ref n="Cic. Arch. 17">17</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="revocatum_p162_l5">
								[<foreign xml:lang="lat">hunc</foreign>], subject of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="dicere_p162_l5">.
								The <foreign xml:lang="fra">encore</foreign> was a common Roman
								practice, as with us. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sic_p162_l10">, <gloss>
								this</gloss> (referring to the indir. disc. that follows). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ennius_p162_l14">:
								the father of Latin poetry. He was born at Rudiae in Magna Graecia (<date when="-0239">B.C. 239</date>), but wrote in Latin. His principal
								work was the <title xml:lang="lat">Annales</title>, an epic poem upon
								Roman history, lost except for a few fragments. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="bestiae_p162_l19">,
								etc.: alluding to the myths of Orpheus and Arion (see Ovid, Met. 10.3;
								Fasti, 2. 83-118; Virg., Ecl. 8.56). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Homerum_p162_l21">,
								etc.: the names of the cities which thus claimed Homer are given in the
								following hexameter verse: <quote>
									<l>Smyrna, Chios, Colophon, Salamis, Rhodes, Argos, Athenae.</l>
								</quote>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="olim_p162_l27">,
								almost equiv. to an adj.: Cf. § <ref n="AG 321">321</ref>, d (188, e);
								G. 439, N.4; H. 497, 5 (359, N.4); H.-B. 295, C. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Cimbricas_res_p162_l29">:
								the war with the Cimbri and Teutones, who invaded Italy and were at
								length defeated by Marius (the Teutones, <date when="-0102">B.C. 102</date>;
								the Cimbri, 101). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="durior_p162_l30">:
								Marius was a rude and illiterate soldier. The illustration (p.163) shows
								what seems to be the most trustworthy portrait of Marius (from the
								impression of a coin, now lost); various busts have been identified with
								him, but without any probable evidence. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Themistoclem_p163_l3">:
								the great Athenian statesman and general, who won the battle of Salamis
								in the second Persian invasion (<date when="-0480">B.C. 480</date>) and
								afterwards, by his skillful policy, raised Athens to its greatest height
								of power. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="For_section_p163_l8">,
								see Pompey's Military Command </gloss>
lemma: For the statements in this section

content:
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="natura_regione_p163_l14">:
								hendiadys. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ejusdem_p163_l17">,
								i.e. Lucullus. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nostra_p163_l19">, <gloss>as
								ours</gloss> (predicate), agreeing with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="pugna_p163_l19">.
								Cicero means that these exploits, since they have been immortalized by
								Archias, will always remain the glory of the Roman people. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quae_p164_l3">, <gloss>these
								things</gloss> (just mentioned); </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quorum_p164_l3">
								limits </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ingeniis_p164_l3">
								and refers to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="eis_p164_l3">. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Africano_superiori_p164_l4">:
								the conqueror of Hannibal. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_Scipionum_p164_l5">:
								this tomb on the Appian Way has been discovered, and in it a bust of <foreign xml:lang="lat">peperino</foreign> (not marble), which has by some
								been supposed to be that here referred to. It now stands upon the
								sarcophagus of Scipio in the Vatican museum (Fig. 42). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hujus_p164_l8">:
								M. Porcius Cato, later called <foreign xml:lang="lat">Uticensis</foreign>,
								from his killing himself at Utica after Caesar's victory. Cato the
								Censor was his great-grandfather. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Maximi_p164_l10">,
								etc.: Q. Fabius Maximus, "the shield of Rome," in the Second Punic War;
								M. Marcellus, "the sword of Rome" (see note on <ref n="pl48.5">p. 48, l.
								5</ref>); Q. Fulvius Flaccus, a distinguished officer in the same war. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="illum_p164_l11">,
								i.e. Ennius. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Heracliensem_p164_l13">:
								Heraclia (see note on <ref n="pl157.11">p. 157, l. 11</ref>), as being
								an important city, is here contrasted with the insignificant Rudiae. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="civitatibus_p164_l13">
								. § <ref n="AG 375">375</ref> (232 a), G 354. H. 431, 2 (388, 1);
								H.-B. 373, 2. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Graeca_leguntur_p164_l18">
								, <gloss>Greek is read</gloss>. Greek was, in the ancient world, almost
								the universal language of polite society; cf. the use of French in
								modern times. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quo_p164_l21"> (<gloss>
								whither</gloss>) relates to <foreign xml:lang="lat">eodem</foreign> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">thither</foreign>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cupere_p164_l21">
								governs the clause </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quo_penetrare_p164_l21">
								. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="populis_p165_l1">,
								dat. after <foreign xml:lang="lat">ampla</foreign>, <gloss>a noble thing
								for them</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="eis_p165_l2">,
								i.e. the individuals by whom these exploits are performed as contrasted
								with their peoples as a whole. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Magnus_p165_l10">,
								i.e. Pompey. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="esset_p165_l17">: § <ref n="AG 517">517</ref>,0 (308,a); B. 304, 2 G. 597, R.1; H. 579, I
								(510, N.2); H.-B. 581. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="civitate_donaretur_p165_l17">:
								§ <ref n="AG 364">364</ref> (225, d); B.187, i, a; G. 348; H. 426, 6
								(384, ii, 2); H.-B. 365, b. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="donaret_p165_l18">,
								sc. <foreign xml:lang="lat">civitate</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="repudiasset_p165_l19">:
								the <foreign xml:lang="lat">protasis</foreign> is implied in <foreign xml:lang="lat">petentem</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quem_p165_l19">,
								subject of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="jubere_p165_l19">,
								below. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="de_populo_p165_l20">
								, <gloss>of the people</gloss>, i.e. of low birth. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_fecisset_p165_l20">
								, <gloss>which he had made as an epigram</gloss> (poetical address) <gloss>to
								him</gloss>; for gender, see § <ref n="AG 296">296</ref>, a (195, d);
								B. 250, 3; cf. G. 211, R.5 H. 396, 2 (445, 4); H.-B. 326, I. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tantummodo_longiusculis_p165_l21">
								, <gloss>merely with the alternate verses a little longer</gloss>, i.e.
								it was written in some metre in which (as in elegiac verse) long and
								short lines alternated; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tantummodo_p165_l21">
								implies that this was its only merit. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="eis_rebus_p165_l22">,
								i.e. confiscated goods. Apparently a commander could take out from the
								booty anything he desired to bestow upon a soldier as a reward; and here
								the confiscated goods are treated in the same manner. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="pingue_peregrinum_p165_l30">,
								cognate accusatives: § <ref n="AG 390">390</ref>,a(240,a); B.
								176,2,b,N.; G.333,2,N.6; H.409 and 1 (371,ii); H.-B. 396, 2. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="prae_ferendum_p166_l2">
								, <gloss>a thing to be proud of</gloss>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="optimus_quisque_p166_l3">:
								§ <ref n="AG 313">313</ref>, b (93,c); B. 252,5, c; G. 318,2; H. 515,
								2 (458, I); H.-B. 278, 2, b. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_ipso_p166_l5">
								, <gloss>in the very act</gloss>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="praedicari_p166_l6">:
								impersonal. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Brutus_p166_l7">:
								D. Junius Brutus (cos. <date when="-0138">B.C. 138</date>) conquered the
								Lusitanians (of Portugal). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Acci_p166_l8">: <gloss>L.
								Accius</gloss> (less properly Attius), a tragic poet (born <date when="-0170">B.C. 170</date>); distinguished for vigor and
								sublimity; he lived long enough for Cicero in his youth to converse with
								him. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Fulvius_p166_l10">:
								M. Fulvius Nobilior (cos. <date when="-0189">B.C. 189</date>) subdued
								Aetolia. He was distinguished as a friend of Greek literature, and
								built, from the spoils of war, a temple to Hercules and the Muses. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="prope_armati_p166_l12">
								, <gloss>having scarce laid aside their arms</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="togati_p166_l14">:
								see note on <ref n="pl125.17">p. 125, l. 17</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quas_res_p166_l18">,
								i.e. the suppression of Catiline's conspiracy. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="adornavi_p166_l23">
								, <gloss>I supplied him</gloss> with materials (i.e. facts). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quid_quod_p166_l25">,
								etc.: § <ref n="AG 531">531</ref>, 2 (317, 2); B. 282, 2; G. 631, 2;
								H. 590 (497, 1); H.-B. 513, 2. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nec_tantis_p166_l30">,
								etc.: here the apod. begins. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="parvi_animi_p167_l5">
								, <gloss>mean-spirited</gloss>: § <ref n="AG 345">345</ref> (215); B.
								203, I; G. 365; H. 440, 3 (394, v); H.-B. 355 </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="imagines_p167_l9">
								, <gloss>busts</gloss>. Whoever held any <foreign xml:lang="lat">curule</foreign>
								office (dictator, consul, interrex, praetor, curule aedile) thereby
								secured to his posterity the <foreign xml:lang="lat">jus imaginum</foreign>,
								i.e. the right to place in their halls and carry in funeral processions
								a wax mask of him as well as of any other deceased members of the family
								of curule rank. Since this right was a distinguishing mark of the Roman
								nobility, it was naturally highly prized. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="esset_p165_l17">: § <ref n="AG 517">517</ref>,0 (308,a); B. 304, 2 G. 597, R.1; H. 579, I
								(510, N.2); H.-B. 581. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="civitate_donaretur_p165_l17">:
								§ <ref n="AG 364">364</ref> (225, d); B.187, i, a; G. 348; H. 426, 6
								(384, ii, 2); H.-B. 365, b. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="donaret_p165_l18">,
								sc. <foreign xml:lang="lat">civitate</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="repudiasset_p165_l19">:
								the <foreign xml:lang="lat">protasis</foreign> is implied in <foreign xml:lang="lat">petentem</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quem_p165_l19">,
								subject of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="jubere_p165_l19">,
								below. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="de_populo_p165_l20">
								, <gloss>of the people</gloss>, i.e. of low birth. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_fecisset_p165_l20">
								, <gloss>which he had made as an epigram</gloss> (poetical address) <gloss>to
								him</gloss>; for gender, see § <ref n="AG 296">296</ref>, a (195, d);
								B. 250, 3; cf. G. 211, R.5 H. 396, 2 (445, 4); H.-B. 326, I. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tantummodo_longiusculis_p165_l21">
								, <gloss>merely with the alternate verses a little longer</gloss>, i.e.
								it was written in some metre in which (as in elegiac verse) long and
								short lines alternated; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tantummodo_p165_l21">
								implies that this was its only merit. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="eis_rebus_p165_l22">,
								i.e. confiscated goods. Apparently a commander could take out from the
								booty anything he desired to bestow upon a soldier as a reward; and here
								the confiscated goods are treated in the same manner. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="pingue_peregrinum_p165_l30">,
								cognate accusatives: § <ref n="AG 390">390</ref>,a(240,a); B.
								176,2,b,N.; G.333,2,N.6; H.409 and 1 (371,ii); H.-B. 396, 2. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="prae_ferendum_p166_l2">
								, <gloss>a thing to be proud of</gloss>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="optimus_quisque_p166_l3">:
								§ <ref n="AG 313">313</ref>, b (93,c); B. 252,5, c; G. 318,2; H. 515,
								2 (458, I); H.-B. 278, 2, b. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_ipso_p166_l5">
								, <gloss>in the very act</gloss>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="praedicari_p166_l6">:
								impersonal. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Brutus_p166_l7">:
								D. Junius Brutus (cos. <date when="-0138">B.C. 138</date>) conquered the
								Lusitanians (of Portugal). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Acci_p166_l8">: <gloss>L.
								Accius</gloss> (less properly Attius), a tragic poet (born <date when="-0170">B.C. 170</date>); distinguished for vigor and
								sublimity; he lived long enough for Cicero in his youth to converse with
								him. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Fulvius_p166_l10">:
								M. Fulvius Nobilior (cos. <date when="-0189">B.C. 189</date>) subdued
								Aetolia. He was distinguished as a friend of Greek literature, and
								built, from the spoils of war, a temple to Hercules and the Muses. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="prope_armati_p166_l12">
								, <gloss>having scarce laid aside their arms</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="togati_p166_l14">:
								see note on <ref n="pl125.17">p. 125, l. 17</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quas_res_p166_l18">,
								i.e. the suppression of Catiline's conspiracy. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="adornavi_p166_l23">
								, <gloss>I supplied him</gloss> with materials (i.e. facts). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quid_quod_p166_l25">,
								etc.: § <ref n="AG 531">531</ref>, 2 (317, 2); B. 282, 2; G. 631, 2;
								H. 590 (497, 1); H.-B. 513, 2. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nec_tantis_p166_l30">,
								etc.: here the apod. begins. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="parvi_animi_p167_l5">
								, <gloss>mean-spirited</gloss>: § <ref n="AG 345">345</ref> (215); B.
								203, I; G. 365; H. 440, 3 (394, v); H.-B. 355 </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="imagines_p167_l9">
								, <gloss>busts</gloss>. Whoever held any <foreign xml:lang="lat">curule</foreign>
								office (dictator, consul, interrex, praetor, curule aedile) thereby
								secured to his posterity the <foreign xml:lang="lat">jus imaginum</foreign>,
								i.e. the right to place in their halls and carry in funeral processions
								a wax mask of him as well as of any other deceased members of the family
								of curule rank. Since this right was a distinguishing mark of the Roman
								nobility, it was naturally highly prized. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="pudore_eo_p167_l20">
								, <gloss>of
									such high character</gloss> (i.e. sense of honor and self-respect,
								as contrasted with the unprincipled Greek hangers-on with whom Roman
								society was infested). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vetustate_p167_l22">,
								i.e. long-continued friendship (see sect. <ref n="Cic. Arch. 5">5</ref>
								). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="id_convenit_p167_l22">
								, <gloss>it is fitting</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="videatis_p167_l24">,
								subj. of integral part. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="municipi_p167_l25">,
								i.e. Heraclia. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="comprobetur_p167_l26">,
								subj. of characteristic. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_p167_l28">, with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="accipiatis_p167_l28">
								, <ref n="pl168.2">p. 168, l. 2</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ex_numero_p168_l1">,
								i.e. of poets. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ab_qui_p168_l9">,
								etc.: Q. Cicero (see Introd.). </gloss>
